mission.title: Install and Configure Runme Notebooks on Kubernetes
mission.description: Runme is a tool that allows you to execute code snippets directly from your markdown files, making it easier to follow operational documentation and runbooks. Installing Runme enables you to create runnable instructions in your documentation, enhancing reliability and usability.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Runme Helm repository to your local Helm setup with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add runme https://runmedev.github.io/runme
```

steps.title: Install Runme using Helm
steps.description:
Install the Runme application in your Kubernetes cluster using Helm. Replace `<namespace>` with your desired namespace:
```bash
helm install runme runme/runme --namespace <namespace> --create-namespace --version v3.16.5
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Runme pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <namespace>
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the Runme deployment to set resource limits. Use the following command to open the deployment in your editor:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment runme --namespace <namespace>
```
Add the following resource limits under the `spec.containers` section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
```

steps.title: Access the Runme service
steps.description:
Port-forward the Runme service to access it locally:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/runme --namespace <namespace> 8080:80
```
You can now access Runme at http://localhost:8080.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Runme will show the Runme pods in a running state when you check with `kubectl get pods`. You will also be able to access the Runme service locally via port forwarding.

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Runme release from your Kubernetes cluster using Helm. Replace `<namespace>` with your actual namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall runme --namespace <namespace>
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Runme. Execute the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd runmes.runme.dev
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace <namespace>
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the namespace if it is no longer needed and verify that it has been removed. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace <namespace>
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of the Runme deployment. You can use the following command to export the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get deployment runme --namespace <namespace> -o yaml > runme-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Runme installation to the latest version. Execute the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade runme runme/runme --namespace <namespace> --version v3.16.6
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Runme pods are running the new version successfully. Use the following command:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Runme pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors. Use the following command to view the logs:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace <namespace>
``` 
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Runme service, verify that the service is running and correctly configured. Check the service status with:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace <namespace>
``` 
Ensure that the port-forward command is running and that you are using the correct port.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If the pods are being killed due to Out Of Memory (OOM) errors, you may need to adjust the resource limits. Check the pod status with:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace <namespace>
``` 
Look for OOMKilled messages and consider increasing the memory limit in the deployment.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails due to a version conflict, ensure that you are using a compatible version. Check the current version with:
```bash
helm list --namespace <namespace>
``` 
You may need to roll back to the previous version if the new version is incompatible:
```bash
helm rollback runme <revision-number> --namespace <namespace>
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
